text: True primary cysts of the pancreas with a lining of epithelium are rare. They usually originate with an adenoma.
While serious cyst adenomas usually remain benign, mucinous cyst adenomas often become malignant.
These cysts should be removed with the part of the pancreas containing the adenoma with limited resection into healthy pancreatic tissue.
When the left part of the pancreas is involved, the preservation of the spleen is a particular challenge.

Because of the organ's important immunological function and the permanent immune deficiency toward encapsulated cocci resulting from its removal, the spleen should also be preserved when the tail of the pancreas is removed laparoscopically.
This case involves a twenty-year-old woman with a symptomatic cyst with a diameter of ten centimeters. CT was highly suggestive of a cystadenoma.
The surgeon inserts a 12mm working trocar in the left infraumbilical position along the medial clavicular line, and a 5mm trocar in the epigastrium to the right of the midline.
The surgeon inserts the trocar for the optic using an open technique, positioning it infraumbilically.
The surgeon uses an ultra shear to separate the omentum from the transverse colon, creating a wide opening into the omental bursa.
The surgeon presents the cystic tumor, measuring ten centimeters in diameter, located on the tail of the pancreas. Adhesions surrounding the tumor are being carefully separated.
The surgeon identifies the corpus and parenchymal portions that are attached to the cystic wall in certain areas.

A flexible retractor is inserted through a five millimeter trocar to allow further dorsal preparation of the tumor. The retractor helps in repositioning the tumor, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon carefully severs adhesions from the dorsal and retropancreatic directions, progressively disconnecting the tissue from the splenic hilus.
The surgeon identifies the splenic vein on the dorsal surface of the cyst and carefully severs the small branch veins connecting the pancreas to the splenic vein.

The surgeon severs the pancreatic branches and branches of the splenic artery on the portion of the pancreas designated for removal, ensuring precise devascularization limited to the resection line to prevent necrosis and fistula formation.
The surgeon uses an endless stapler loaded with a musculoskeletal cartridge to safely resect the tail of the pancreas and the adjacent cystic tumor.
The surgeon uses an ultra shear device to sever any remaining small adhesions around the surgical site, ensuring complete separation of the tissue.
The surgeon rinses and cleans the tumor bed, then seals the cut edge of the tail of the pancreas with fibrin adhesive to protect against fistulas.
The surgeon removes the tumor through the infraumbilical trocar opening using an appropriately sized retrieval bag.
Histology later confirmed the suspected preoperative diagnosis of a mucinous cystadenoma.
Both the cosmetic results and the CT, a three-month follow-up, were highly satisfactory.
